source: Rocky Linux Admin Guide, ch. 13.5.4-13.7 lookup, records, troubleshooting (pp. 248-255)

questions.q: Which three commands view DNS resolution results, and which is recommended?
options: host, nslookup and dig - dig is the recommended one
ping, traceroute and mtr - mtr recommended
ss, netstat and ip - ip recommended
getent, hostnamectl and nmcli - getent recommended
questions.why: host, nslookup and dig all query DNS; dig is the recommended tool.

questions.q: Look up the DNS records for wiki.rockylinux.org using the recommended tool.
questions.why: dig <name> queries DNS and shows the answer section (e.g. CNAME/A records).

questions.q: What is the difference between an A record and an AAAA record?
options: A resolves a name to an IPv4 address; AAAA resolves it to an IPv6 address
A is IPv6; AAAA is IPv4
A is for mail; AAAA is for web
questions.why: A maps a name to IPv4, AAAA maps it to IPv6.

questions.q: What does a CNAME record do?
options: Resolves a domain name to ANOTHER domain name (an alias)
Maps an IP address to a name
questions.why: CNAME points one name at another name (an alias); PTR does the reverse IP-to-name mapping.

questions.q: What is an MX record for?
options: Specifying the e-mail server(s) for a domain
Mapping an IP to a hostname
questions.why: MX names the mail server(s) handling a domain's e-mail; NS lists the DNS servers, TXT holds text (SPF etc.).

questions.q: Which record type maps an IP address back to a domain name (reverse resolution)?
questions.why: PTR maps IP to name and is used for reverse resolution (e.g. by mail servers).

questions.q: Which tool helps diagnose an IP or MAC address conflict on the network?
questions.why: arp-scan (from EPEL) scans the LAN and flags duplicate addresses (DUP), which reveal IP/MAC conflicts - common with cloned VMs.
